summary: Command-backed CPU boost tweak that uses the documented PERFBOOSTMODE power setting instead of the old raw registry bundle. Microsoft publishes PERFBOOSTMODE as a supported powercfg surface, and the guest reversible probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed the active scheme can be read, moved to 1 and 2, and restored back to the original state.

setting.name: Optimize CPU Performance Boost
setting.casual_explanation: This tweak uses the documented powercfg PERFBOOSTMODE surface to push the active power plan toward a more aggressive CPU boost profile.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change it when they want a more aggressive boost profile on performance-focused systems and are willing to trade extra power use or heat for responsiveness.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: More aggressive boost behavior can help burst performance, but it can also increase heat and power use. The important part here is that the app now uses an official Microsoft powercfg surface instead of an undocumented registry bundle.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents PERFBOOSTMODE as a supported power setting with indexes 0 through 4, where 2 is Aggressive. The app now uses a command-backed implementation: detect via powercfg.exe /qh SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_PROCESSOR PERFBOOSTMODE, apply via /setacvalueindex and /setdcvalueindex with PERFBOOSTMODE=2, and restore the previously detected AC/DC values on rollback. A guest-side reversible probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed the surface is live on this build.

targets.path: powercfg.exe /setacvalueindex SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_PROCESSOR PERFBOOSTMODE 2 /setdcvalueindex SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_PROCESSOR PERFBOOSTMODE 2 /setactive SCHEME_CURRENT

allowed_values.label: Disabled
allowed_values.meaning: PowerCfg alias PERFBOOSTMODE index 0 disables boosting.

allowed_values.label: Enabled
allowed_values.meaning: PowerCfg alias PERFBOOSTMODE index 1 enables boosting.

allowed_values.label: Aggressive
allowed_values.meaning: PowerCfg alias PERFBOOSTMODE index 2 enables aggressive boosting.

allowed_values.label: Same as 1
allowed_values.meaning: PowerCfg alias PERFBOOSTMODE index 3 behaves the same as index 1.

allowed_values.label: Same as 2
allowed_values.meaning: PowerCfg alias PERFBOOSTMODE index 4 behaves the same as index 2.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app implementation is command-backed. It detects the current active power plan's PERFBOOSTMODE values, applies PERFBOOSTMODE=2 through powercfg, verifies the live state, and restores the previously detected AC/DC values on rollback.

writes.path: powercfg.exe /setacvalueindex SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_PROCESSOR PERFBOOSTMODE 2 /setdcvalueindex SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_PROCESSOR PERFBOOSTMODE 2 /setactive SCHEME_CURRENT
writes.notes: Detected state is serialized as the previous AC/DC indices and restored on rollback.

windows_defaults.label: Windows-managed boost baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows systems using normal processor boost management

states.rationale: The active scheme can vary by machine, so the current PERFBOOSTMODE state should be observed rather than assumed.

windows_defaults.notes: Use powercfg /qh SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_PROCESSOR PERFBOOSTMODE to observe the current state before applying a change.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems using supported Windows processor boost management
avoid_for: Systems where the operator explicitly wants a more aggressive boost profile

states.rationale: Keep boost behavior under the normal Windows-managed baseline.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want supported Windows processor boost behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are trying to force aggressive boost tuning without a specific performance reason.
tradeoffs: Windows keeps its supported boost-management path.
No explicit boost override is pushed through powercfg.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: PERFBOOSTMODE
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents PERFBOOSTMODE as a supported power setting and shows the index table, including Aggressive at index 2.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider now exposes the command-backed CPU boost tweak instead of the old raw registry bundle.

evidence.title: Command-backed CPU boost implementation
evidence.summary: The engine tweak detects PERFBOOSTMODE with powercfg /qh, applies PERFBOOSTMODE=2 on both AC and DC through powercfg /setacvalueindex and /setdcvalueindex, and restores the detected indices on rollback.

evidence.title: Guest reversible probe for PERFBOOSTMODE
evidence.summary: Win25H2Clean guest reversible probe confirmed BEFORE_AC=2, BEFORE_DC=2, AFTER_1_AC=1, AFTER_1_DC=1, AFTER_2_AC=2, AFTER_2_DC=2, RESTORED_AC=2, and RESTORED_DC=2.

decision.why: Microsoft documents PERFBOOSTMODE as a supported powercfg surface with Aggressive at index 2, the app now uses that documented surface directly, and a guest reversible probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed the active scheme can move through 1 and 2 and restore back to the original state.

validation_proof.notes: The guest reversible probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed the live surface on this build with BEFORE_AC=2, BEFORE_DC=2, AFTER_1_AC=1, AFTER_1_DC=1, AFTER_2_AC=2, AFTER_2_DC=2, RESTORED_AC=2, and RESTORED_DC=2.
